content:
6 min read
Just now

--

Remember October 2025? When AWS US-East-1 went down and took half the internet with it? If you work in DevOps or SRE, you probably spent that day explaining to leadership why your “highly available” system was very much unavailable.
Here’s what should worry everyone: Most of those systems were passing their chaos tests.
The pods were running. The health checks were green. The load balancers showed healthy. But users couldn’t check out. API calls were timing out. The service was technically alive and functionally dead.
We’d been asking the wrong question for years.
The Binary Trap
Traditional chaos engineering gives you a thumbs up or thumbs down. You kill a pod, it comes back, test passes. Ship it.
But in production, “it came back” means nothing if it:

• Took 5 minutes to recover

• Dropped 50% of transactions during that window

• Caused a cascade failure in your payment service

• Spiked latency to 3 seconds for the next hour
All of those scenarios get marked as PASS in traditional tooling.
This isn’t theoretical. During the 2023 GitHub outage, their health checks were green while users couldn’t push code for 2 hours. The systems were “up” according to traditional monitoring. User experience said otherwise.
The test passed. But “passed” isn’t the same as “your users didn’t notice.”
How Krkn’s Resiliency Score Changes the Game
Krkn is an open-source chaos engineering tool for Kubernetes that recently added a feature that shifts the conversation: Resiliency Scoring. Instead of pass/fail, you get a number from 0–100 that tells you exactly how much your system degraded.
Not a vibe. Not a health score pulled from thin air. A calculated metric based on the SLOs you already care about.
Here’s how it works.
Step 1: Define What Actually Matters
Instead of just checking if pods are running, you define Prometheus queries that measure user experience in your alerts file:
# Business-critical: Payment processing must stay available
- expr: up{job="payment-api"} == 0
description: Payment API is completely down
severity: critical
weight: 20# High-priority: API latency
- expr: histogram_quantile(0.99, sum(rate(http_request_duration_seconds_bucket[5m])) by (le)) > 1
description: 99th percentile API latency exceeds 1s
severity: warning
weight: 5# Standard critical: etcd health (uses default weight of 3)
- expr: etcd_server_has_leader{job=~".*etcd.*"} == 0
description: etcd cluster has no leader
severity: criticalStep 2: Weight Your Pain Points
Not all failures hurt equally. A broken recommendation engine is annoying. A broken checkout button is a P0.
Krkn supports two weighting approaches:
Default severity-based weights:

• Critical SLOs = 3 points each

• Warning SLOs = 1 point each
Custom weights for business-critical scenarios:

• Payment API down = 20 points (catastrophic)

• Auth service down = 15 points (very critical)

• High API latency = 5 points (important warning)

• Low disk space = 1 point (standard warning)
This lets you create a scoring model that reflects your actual business priorities.
Step 3: Run Chaos, Get Math
Krkn runs your chaos scenario — let’s say you kill the payment service pod. During the chaos window, it queries Prometheus to check all your SLOs.
The score is pure math:
Score = (Points Earned / Total Possible Points) × 100Let’s say you have:

• Payment API (weight 20): ✗ Failed

• Auth service (weight 15): ✓ Passed

• etcd leader (weight 3): ✓ Passed

• API latency (weight 5): ✓ Passed
Total Points = 20 + 15 + 3 + 5 = 43
Points Lost = 20
Points Earned = 23Score = (23 / 43) × 100 = 53%A score of 53% tells you: “Your payment system is down. Nothing else matters.”
Compare that to a green checkmark that says “PASS” ✓.
Why This Catches Real Problems
Here’s what a binary pass/fail test misses:
Scenario A: Payment service pod dies. New pod spins up in 2 seconds. Error rate stays at 0.2%. Latency bumps from 150ms to 180ms. Traditional result: PASS ✓ Krkn score: 98% What actually happened: Users didn’t notice anything.
Scenario B: Payment service pod dies. New pod spins up in 90 seconds. Error rate spikes to 8% during recovery. Half your checkout attempts fail. Traditional result: PASS ✓ Krkn score: 45% What actually happened: You just lost revenue and customer trust.
Both scenarios get the same green checkmark. One is fine. One is a production incident waiting to happen.
This is exactly what happened during Stripe’s 2019 outage. Their internal chaos tests showed systems were resilient. But when a real failure hit, cascading timeouts caused a 2+ hours outage affecting thousands of businesses. The individual components recovered. The user experience didn’t.
A resiliency score would have caught this. A score of 45% for a payment system isn’t shippable. A green checkmark gives you false confidence.
The “No Data” Problem Krkn Solved
One technical challenge: What happens when Prometheus returns no data?
This happens constantly with alerting queries. If you have a query like rate(errors[5m]) > threshold, it only returns data when there's a problem. No errors = no data returned.
If you marked “no data” as a failure, your score would tank every time a metric didn’t exist, which is most of the time for well-behaved systems.
Krkn’s approach:

• Data shows breach → SLO fails, score drops

• No data returned → SLO passes
This matches how Prometheus alerting already works and prevents false positives from killing your CI/CD pipeline.
Scenario-Based Scoring: Where krknctl Comes In
For multi-scenario chaos runs, Krkn supports per-scenario resiliency scoring with weighted aggregation. This is where krknctl (Krkn’s CLI orchestration tool) really shines.
krknctl can:

• Run multiple chaos scenarios in parallel (up to 50+ at once)

• Assign different weights to different scenarios

• Calculate a weighted average across all scenarios

• Generate randomized chaos tests (expand 3 scenarios into 100+ variations)
The weighted average formula:
Final Score = Σ(scenario_score × scenario_weight) / Σ(scenario_weight)Example:

• Pod delete scenario (weight 1.0): Score 95%

• Network partition (weight 2.0): Score 75%

• Node failure (weight 3.0): Score 60%
Final Score = (95×1 + 75×2 + 60×3) / (1+2+3)
Final Score = (95 + 150 + 180) / 6
Final Score = 425 / 6 = 71%This lets you prioritize critical failure modes. A node failure is 3x more important than a pod delete, so it gets 3x the weight in your final score.
What a Score Actually Tells You
Based on the patterns emerging from the Krkn community:
90–100%: Ship it. Your system degraded gracefully or not at all.
70–89%: Investigate. Something degraded but maybe it’s acceptable. Check if it’s a critical path.
50–69%: Don’t ship. A major component failed. You need better fallback handling.
Below 50%: Your chaos test just simulated a production incident. Fix this before it happens to real users.
The scores are deterministic. Same scenario, same config = same score (±2 points variance). It’s based on your SLOs, not random.
How to Start Using This
If you’re already using Krkn:
# Update to latest version (v2.5+)
pip install krkn --upgrade# Add SLOs to your alerts.yaml file
# Run your chaos test
krkn --config scenarios/pod-delete.yaml# Check the resiliency score in kraken.reportIf you want to orchestrate multiple scenarios with krknctl:
# Run multiple weighted scenarios
krknctl random run scenarios.json --max-parallel 50For newcomers:
Grab Krkn from GitHub and start with the example configs. You can also refer to the resiliency docs.
The Shift We Need to Make
Binary chaos testing made sense when we had 3 microservices and a monolith. Now we have 50 services, 12 databases, and a dependency graph that looks like a bowl of spaghetti.
“Did it survive?” is no longer a useful question.
The useful questions are:

• How badly did it degrade?

• Which SLOs failed?

• Would users have noticed?

• Can we ship this to production?
A resiliency score answers all of them.
The Real Cost of False Confidence
According to Gartner, the average cost of IT downtime is $5,600 per minute. For e-commerce, it can hit $300,000 per hour during peak times.
But here’s what most people miss: The real cost isn’t the downtime itself. It’s shipping systems that you think are resilient but aren’t.
When your chaos tests say “PASS” but your payment system drops 50% of transactions during a pod restart, you’re not measuring resilience. You’re measuring false confidence.
When Netflix tests Chaos Monkey, they don’t just check if services recover. They measure stream start success rates, buffering ratios, and error rates during failures. They know that “it came back online” means nothing if users spent 5 minutes staring at loading spinners.
That’s the standard Krkn is bringing to Kubernetes chaos engineering.
October is Coming
The next major outage is already scheduled. You just don’t know the date yet.
When it happens, you want to find out from your dashboards, not from your users. You want to have already tested the failure mode. You want to know — with a number, not a guess — that your system can handle it.
That’s what the Resiliency Score enables.
Your chaos tests should make you uncomfortable. If they’re all passing with flying colors, you’re probably not testing the right things.
A score of 54% is honest. A score of 54% is fixable. A green checkmark that hides a broken checkout flow is neither.
